perturbation.original_text: Master Distributor shall not, during the term of this Agreement and the Master Distributorship Agreement, and at any and all times following termination, for any reason, of this Agreement or the Master Distributorship Agreement, disclose, use, reveal, report, publish, disclose, transfer, or make available, directly or indirectly, to any person, business concern, or other entity, any Trade Secrets except in the proper performance of its duties hereunder; provided, however, Master Distributor is not prohibited hereby from disclosing or using any Trade Secrets which subsequently becomes part of the public domain through no breach of this Agreement or the Master Distributorship Agreement and through no fault of Master Distributor.
perturbation.changed_text: Master Distributor shall endeavor not to, during the term of this Agreement and the Master Distributorship Agreement, and at any and all times following termination, for any reason, of this Agreement or the Master Distributorship Agreement, disclose, use, reveal, report, publish, disclose, transfer, or make available, directly or indirectly, to any person, business concern, or other entity, any Trade Secrets except in the performance of what it perceives as its duties hereunder; provided, however, Master Distributor is not prohibited hereby from disclosing or using any Trade Secrets which subsequently becomes part of the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor not to' weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, introducing ambiguity and making it less enforceable. Replacing 'proper performance of its duties hereunder' with 'performance of what it perceives as its duties hereunder' introduces a subjective element, allowing Master Distributor to interpret its duties. Removing the requirement that the information becomes public domain through no fault of the Master Distributor could lead to the Master Distributor causing the Trade Secrets to be released into the Public Domain.
perturbation.law_explanation: The South Carolina Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to protect trade secrets and introducing subjective interpretations of duties, this modification could fall short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by the Act and allow the Master Distributor to cause the trade secrets to be released to the Public Domain. This is because the Master Distributor can now decide themselves what is within their reasonable duty, giving them more leeway to allow the Trade Secrets to be released into the Public Domain. The act also states that there must be misappropriation of the trade secret to take action, but if the master distributor releases the trade secret by accident, or by subjective intent, then that act is not misappropriation.

Short title. This chapter may be cited as the "South Carolina Trade Secrets Act". HISTORY: 1992 Act No. 437, Section 3, eff June 15, 1992; 1997 Act No. 38, Section 1, eff May 21, 1997. Effect of Amendment The 1997 amendment rewrote this section. SECTION 39-8-20. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, duties imposed by the common law, statute, contract, license, protective order, or other court or administrative order, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired by mistake or under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Owner" means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to the trade secret is reposed. (4) "Person" means an individual, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (5) "Trade secret" means: (a) information including, but not limited to, a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, product, system, or process, design, prototype, procedure, or code that: (i) derives independent economic value, actual or

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, creating a contradiction with the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act. The original contract stated the 'Master Distributor shall not... disclose...any Trade Secrets', whereas the modified text weakens this obligation to 'Master Distributor shall endeavor not to...disclose...any Trade Secrets'. According to the LLM explanation, the Act requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. By changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor not to' and introducing subjective interpretations of duties ('performance of what it perceives as its duties hereunder'), the modified text creates ambiguity. The Master Distributor now decides themselves what is within their reasonable duty, giving them more leeway to allow the Trade Secrets to be released into the Public Domain. The original language would have forced the Master Distributor to show they performed their duties, according to the language of the contract. The ambiguity created opens the contract to a breach of duty.

perturbation.original_text: Master Distributor shall take all appropriate steps to ensure that the Confidential Information and Trade Secrets and any other similar information and data set forth in this Agreement and the Master Distributorship Agreement are not divulged or disclosed to any unauthorized person.
perturbation.changed_text: Master Distributor shall consider taking steps to ensure that the Confidential Information and Trade Secrets and any other similar information and data set forth in this Agreement and the Master Distributorship Agreement are not likely to be divulged or disclosed to any unauthorized person.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall take all appropriate steps to ensure' with 'shall consider taking steps to ensure' weakens the obligation to actively protect confidential information and trade secrets. Replacing 'are not divulged or disclosed' with 'are not likely to be divulged or disclosed' introduces uncertainty and reduces the standard of care. The 'likely' component reduces the need to guarantee compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The South Carolina Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. 'Considering taking steps' is significantly weaker than 'taking all appropriate steps' and does not meet the 'reasonable measures' standard. The word 'likely' means that there is no guarantee that the trade secrets will not be released into the public domain, thus a lack of intent to preserve the Trade Secrets.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information and trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction. The original text mandates 'shall take all appropriate steps to ensure' confidentiality, while the changed text only requires the distributor to 'consider taking steps to ensure' it. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the standard of care, likely failing to meet the 'reasonable measures' standard as explained by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' in Section 39-8-20 (2) as including the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' which the weakened obligation in the changed text makes more likely. The original text suggests intent to fully preserve Trade Secrets, while the changed text does not.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall mutually approve all promotional material used by Master Distributor in connection with the distribution and marketing of the Products to ensure that Master Distributor properly uses the Marks.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties may attempt to mutually approve promotional material used by Master Distributor in connection with the distribution and marketing of the Products to ensure that Master Distributor attempts to properly use the Marks.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall mutually approve' to 'may attempt to mutually approve' makes the approval process discretionary rather than mandatory. Changing 'properly uses' to 'attempts to properly use' lowers the standard for trademark usage, creating ambiguity and potential for misuse.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act protects trademarks and prevents their misuse. By making trademark approval discretionary and lowering the standard for correct usage, the modified text increases the risk of trademark infringement, which violates the Lanham Act. The act says that trademark infringement is illegal, but that is not necessarily the case if the parties are attempting to properly use the marks, even if it results in infringement.

Effect of extension of protection. 1141j. Dependence of extension of protection to the United States on the underlying international registration. 1141k. Duration, affidavits and fees. 1141l. A
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding trademark usage. The original text mandated mutual approval of promotional material and proper use of trademarks. The modified text makes approval discretionary ('may attempt') and lowers the standard for trademark usage ('attempts to properly use'). This creates a contradiction because the Lanham Act, as explained by the LLM, protects trademarks. While the provided law snippets don't explicitly forbid attempts at proper usage, the overarching theme of the Lanham Act is the protection of trademarks which suggests enforcing proper usage rather than merely attempting it. The change weakens the protection and introduces ambiguity, making it easier for the Master Distributor to misuse the Marks, thereby undermining trademark protection.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding alternative dispute settlement provisions in the Master Distributorship Agreement, any action or proceeding to enforce or interpret this Agreement shall be brought only in the state or federal courts sitting in Charleston County, South Carolina, United States, and Master Distributor hereby irrevocably submits and consents to such exclusive jurisdiction and venue.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding alternative dispute settlement provisions in the Master Distributorship Agreement, any action or proceeding to enforce or interpret this Agreement may preferably be brought in the state or federal courts sitting in Charleston County, South Carolina, United States, and Master Distributor submits and consents to such jurisdiction and venue.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be brought only' to 'may preferably be brought' introduces ambiguity and allows for the possibility of legal action in other jurisdictions. Removing 'irrevocably' weakens the commitment to the specified jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1404 allows a court to transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought. The modified text introduces ambiguity about the agreed-upon venue, making it easier for a party to argue for a change of venue under 28 U.S.C. § 1404, thus going against the original intent of the contract's jurisdiction clause. By removing irrevocably, the master distributor is implying that they did not specifically agree to having legal cases be held only in Charleston County, South Carolina.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding venue, potentially conflicting with the original intent of the jurisdiction clause. By changing 'shall be brought only' to 'may preferably be brought' and removing 'irrevocably,' the contract weakens the commitment to Charleston County, South Carolina, as the exclusive venue. According to the LLM explanation, this makes it easier to argue for a change of venue under 28 U.S.C. § 1404. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 28 U.S. Code § 1404, outlines the conditions under which a court may transfer a civil action to another district. The original contract aimed to prevent such transfers by establishing exclusive jurisdiction. The change undermines this by creating ambiguity, which aligns with the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition: 'Ambiguities occur when a legal statement is vague, leading to multiple interpretations...[making] legal obligations open to dispute.'
